commands: ask lyle about the mile road

events.summary: You set off.

Warden Holt: “The gap in the wire? Officially there's no such thing. Unofficially, I find I study my own boots a great deal this hour of the night. A careful soul could slip toward the fork that way — or back out of it, carrying something I'd rather not have to see. The floodlight throws long, but it throws crooked; there's a wedge of dark by the north post where a low, quiet figure simply isn't, as far as I'm concerned — but only after dark. In daylight the open ground is open ground, and I can't help you there. Mind you stay low, stay quiet, and don't make me look.”
You set off.
Old Lyle: “Newcomer. You've the look of someone after the core. Everyone is, lately. Sit. Ask. Knowing's cheaper here than at the Survey, and worth about what you pay.”

intent.raw: ask lyle about the mile road

events.summary: Old Lyle: “The road? Aye — don't look back on her. Look back and she puts the whole walk behind you again, twice over. I've seen the bootprints loop a dead woman a thousand times round one milepost. That one I'd stake my life on.”

Old Lyle: “The bottoms? They eat gold — that's the old wisdom, anyhow. Keep your coin deep and you'll come through fine. Though mind, I'll be straight with you: I'm a Holdout, I've not carried worked iron down into that dark in thirty years. I'm telling you what I was told, not what I've tested with my own hands. Cross-check me if your life's on it.”

The afternoon light runs long and amber; an hour or so of honest day still in hand. But the dark is coming, and it changes the rules — after dusk the Greywater wakes to the iron you carry, and the deep places grow hungry. If your road runs through them, time it before the light fails.
You set off.
Eun of the Survey: “The Survey trades in knowing. You bring me an observation or a coin, I give you a law you can stake your life on. That is the whole of the arrangement.”
You set off.
You set off.

The sun is on the horizon now and the light is going fast — minutes of true day left, no more. Whatever you mean to do by daylight, do it now: cross the Greywater, or reach the deep, before the dark wakes them. After dusk the bottoms hunger for worked iron, and the deep places for anyone who stops moving.
The road is not counting honestly. The distance behind you is not what it was.
You have seen enough to almost name the rule of The Mile Road. One clear thought might settle it — try: deduce the mile road.
You stop, and turn, and look back the way you came.
The sun gives up without ceremony. In the long shadows the wrong-angled things look almost right, which is worse.
[The Mile Road]
You glance back — and the road behind you yawns suddenly twice as long, the waystation dwindled to a far grey smudge. Something in you slips a notch out of true. (You are Unsettled.)
You set off.
She kept looking back. The road kept giving her more road to look at. She never reached the end of it.
You are certain now. The Mile Road: On the Mile Road, to look back is to double the distance behind you.
You set off.
You set off.
[The Greywater]
The iron in your kit goes soft and rotten-red, slumping toward ore.
Worked iron is rotting here, and quickly. Something in the dark un-makes it.
You have seen enough to almost name the rule of The Greywater. One clear thought might settle it — try: deduce the greywater.
[The Greywater]
A low hum comes up through the soles of your boots — and you feel it answer, faintly, in the iron you carry. The hum is in the metal. It is calling your worked iron back to the ground it came from.
[The Greywater]
You are certain now. The Greywater: In the Greywater after dark, worked metal slumps back toward ore. Iron tools and blades fail.
[The Greywater]
You rest as well as the Zone allows. Hours pass.
Full dark, and no honest dark at all. The Hush comes awake around you, lawful and patient and hungry.
[The Greywater]
You rest as well as the Zone allows. Hours pass.
[The Greywater]
You rest as well as the Zone allows. Hours pass.
[The Greywater]

Your reading of The Mile Road has aged a little — the fine detail going soft at the edges, the way an old memory does. The shape of it you still hold; only the particulars are worth confirming again, when you can.
You rest as well as the Zone allows. Hours pass.
[The Greywater]
You set off.
A grey, grudging not-yet-light gathers at the edges of things. Predawn, the hour the Zone holds its breath.
You set off.
You close your hand around the core. It is warmer than it should be, and it settles into your pack like it has decided to come with you.
Word of the core moves faster than you can. By the time you turn for home, the Cordon will be watching the gate for exactly what rides in your pack — and the Striders will be watching the Cordon.
You set off.
You set off.
You set off.
You set off.
You melt into the dark. The cordon patrol loses the thread of you entirely.
You set off.
